subquestions.question: Show that a process $(X_t)_{t\in \R^+}$ is a Brownian motion if and only if: $X_0=0$, $(X_t)_{t\in \R^+}$ is a centered Gaussian process, ${\rm Cov}(X_s,X_t)=\inf(s,t).$

preconditions: For \(s \leq t\), we decompose \(X_t = X_s + (X_t - X_s)\).
Standard Brownian motion has Gaussian increments
arguments: a linear combination of independent Gaussian increments thus it is Gaussian
atom2.outcome: The process is therefore centered Gaussian

preconditions: Compute $\text{Cov}(X_t - X_s, X_v - X_u)$ for \(s < t \leq u < v\).
arguments: :calculus \[ \text{Cov}(X_t - X_s, X_v - X_u) = \min(t,v) - \min(t,u) - \min(s,v) + \min(s,u) \] This always gives 0, proving that
atom1.outcome: disjoint increments are uncorrelated, hence independent

preconditions: Compute the variance of an increment of $X$
arguments: thus the law depends only on \(t-s\)
outcome: This shows that the increments are stationary

preconditions: the law of increments is Gaussian centered of variance $t-s$, i.e. \(\mathcal{N}(0, t-s)\),
arguments: plus the assumption of continuity of paths or by taking the continuous version via Kolmogorov's theorem)
atom3.outcome: the classical definition of Brownian motion.

subquestions.question: Show that if $W_t$ is a Brownian motion, $ \{X_t=t\;W_{\frac 1t}, t>0, X_0=0 \}$ is a Brownian motion too.

arguments: Hence by the law of large numbers for Brownian motion

preconditions: For any arbitrary \(t_1, \dots, t_n\), the vector \((X_{t_1}, \dots, X_{t_n})\)
arguments: is a linear combination of the \(W_{1/t_i}\)

preconditions: Gaussianity: the vector \((X_{t_1}, \dots, X_{t_n})\) is centered Gaussian for any set of times
arguments: we get by definition of a Brownian motion
